question:
Consider the following game played with a deck of $2n$ cards numbered
from 1 to $2n$. The deck is randomly shuffled and $n$ cards are dealt to
each of two players. Beginning with $A$, the players take turns
discarding one of their remaining cards and announcing its number. The
game ends as soon as the sum of the numbers on the discarded cards is
divisible by $2n+1$. The last person to discard wins the game. Assuming
optimal strategy by both $A$ and $B$, what is the probability that $A$ wins?
solution: Solution. Player \( B \) can always win, because \( B \) can always guarantee that \( A \) will not win on the next move: \( B \) holds one more card than \( A \), and each of \( A \) 's cards causes at most one of \( B \) 's cards to be a fatal play. Hence \( B \) has at least one safe play. Player \( B \) wins on the last move if not earlier, since the sum of the numbers on all the cards is \( n(2 n+1) \).

descriptive_long.question:
Consider the following game played with a deck of $2halfdeck$ cards numbered
from 1 to $2halfdeck$. The deck is randomly shuffled and $halfdeck$ cards are dealt to
each of two players. Beginning with $playera$, the players take turns
discarding one of their remaining cards and announcing its number. The
game ends as soon as the sum of the numbers on the discarded cards is
divisible by $2halfdeck+1$. The last person to discard wins the game. Assuming
optimal strategy by both $playera$ and $playerb$, what is the probability that $playera$ wins?
descriptive_long.solution: Solution. Player \( playerb \) can always win, because \( playerb \) can always guarantee that \( playera \) will not win on the next move: \( playerb \) holds one more card than \( playera \), and each of \( playera \) 's cards causes at most one of \( playerb \) 's cards to be a fatal play. Hence \( playerb \) has at least one safe play. Player \( playerb \) wins on the last move if not earlier, since the sum of the numbers on all the cards is \( halfdeck(2 halfdeck+1) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Consider the following game played with a deck of $2caterpillar$ cards numbered
from 1 to $2caterpillar$. The deck is randomly shuffled and $caterpillar$ cards are dealt to
each of two players. Beginning with $lighthouse$, the players take turns
discarding one of their remaining cards and announcing its number. The
game ends as soon as the sum of the numbers on the discarded cards is
divisible by $2caterpillar+1$. The last person to discard wins the game. Assuming
optimal strategy by both $lighthouse$ and $marshmallow$, what is the probability that $lighthouse$ wins?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution: Solution. Player \( marshmallow \) can always win, because \( marshmallow \) can always guarantee that \( lighthouse \) will not win on the next move: \( marshmallow \) holds one more card than \( lighthouse \), and each of \( lighthouse \) 's cards causes at most one of \( marshmallow \) 's cards to be a fatal play. Hence \( marshmallow \) has at least one safe play. Player \( marshmallow \) wins on the last move if not earlier, since the sum of the numbers on all the cards is \( caterpillar(2 caterpillar+1) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Consider the following game played with a deck of $2limitless$ cards numbered
from 1 to $2limitless$. The deck is randomly shuffled and $limitless$ cards are dealt to
each of two players. Beginning with $audience$, the players take turns
discarding one of their remaining cards and announcing its number. The
game ends as soon as the sum of the numbers on the discarded cards is
divisible by $2limitless+1$. The last person to discard wins the game.
Assuming optimal strategy by both $audience$ and $spectator$, what is
the probability that $audience$ wins?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution: Solution. Player \( spectator \) can always win, because \( spectator \) can always guarantee that \( audience \) will not win on the next move: \( spectator \) holds one more card than \( audience \), and each of \( audience \) 's cards causes at most one of \( spectator \) 's cards to be a fatal play. Hence \( spectator \) has at least one safe play. Player \( spectator \) wins on the last move if not earlier, since the sum of the numbers on all the cards is \( limitless(2 limitless+1) \).

garbled_string.question:
Consider the following game played with a deck of $2qzxwvtnp$ cards numbered
from 1 to $2qzxwvtnp$. The deck is randomly shuffled and $qzxwvtnp$ cards are dealt to
each of two players. Beginning with $hjgrksla$, the players take turns
discarding one of their remaining cards and announcing its number. The
game ends as soon as the sum of the numbers on the discarded cards is
divisible by $2qzxwvtnp+1$. The last person to discard wins the game. Assuming
optimal strategy by both $hjgrksla$ and $pqowieur$, what is the probability that $hjgrksla$ wins?
garbled_string.solution: Solution. Player \( pqowieur \) can always win, because \( pqowieur \) can always guarantee that \( hjgrksla \) will not win on the next move: \( pqowieur \) holds one more card than \( hjgrksla \), and each of \( hjgrksla \)'s cards causes at most one of \( pqowieur \)'s cards to be a fatal play. Hence \( pqowieur \) has at least one safe play. Player \( pqowieur \) wins on the last move if not earlier, since the sum of the numbers on all the cards is \( qzxwvtnp(2 qzxwvtnp+1) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let k be a positive integer.  A deck contains 6k distinct cards numbered 1,2,\ldots ,6k.  After a random shuffle 3k cards are dealt to Peggy and the remaining 3k cards to Quentin.  Peggy moves first. 

On each turn the player whose turn it is chooses one of her/his remaining cards, discards it face-up and announces its number.  Keep a running total of all announced numbers.  The game stops as soon as this running total is divisible by 6k+1, and the player who made that most recent discard wins the game.

Assuming optimal play by both contestants, what is the probability that Peggy (the first player) wins?
kernel_variant.solution:
Write M = 6k + 1 and keep every running total modulo M.

1.  Notation
   *  After t turns let S_t (mod M) be the current total of the discarded numbers.
   *  Just before Quentin's (the second player's) move the two hands contain
         r   cards for Peggy and
         r+1 cards for Quentin,   
     because Peggy has just played.
     Denote these hands by A (Peggy) and B (Quentin).

2.  When can Peggy win on her next move?
   If Quentin now discards a card b\in B, the new total becomes
         S_{t+1} \equiv  S_t + b (mod M).
   Peggy will win on her immediately following move precisely when she owns a card
         c such that S_{t+1}+c \equiv  0 (mod M),
   i.e.
         c \equiv  -S_{t+1} \equiv  M - S_t - b   (mod M).            (1)
   For the fixed current value S_t and each candidate b we therefore define
         f(b) :=  M - S_t - b   (taken in {1,2,\ldots ,6k}).
   Peggy can win after b is played \Leftrightarrow  f(b) is in A.

3.  A counting fact guaranteeing Quentin a `safe' card
   The map  f : B \to  {1,\ldots ,6k}  is injective:  if b_1 \neq  b_2 then
         f(b_1) = M - S_t - b_1 \neq  M - S_t - b_2 = f(b_2).
   Hence the r+1 different cards of B are sent to r+1 different values f(b).
   But Peggy's hand A contains only r cards, so not all f(b) can lie in A.
   Therefore
         There exists at least one card b* \in  B for which f(b*) \notin  A.       (2)

4.  Quentin's optimal strategy
   On every move Quentin does the following.
   Step Q1   If some card b \in  B satisfies S_t + b \equiv  0 (mod M) he plays that card and wins immediately.
   Step Q2   Otherwise he chooses the card b* guaranteed by (2) and discards it.
             Because f(b*) \notin  A, relation (1) shows that Peggy does **not** own the
             unique card that would give her victory on her next turn.  Hence she
             cannot win immediately.

5.  Why Peggy can never win
   Inductively, Quentin's strategy ensures after each of his turns that the running
   total is non-zero and that Peggy lacks a winning reply, so she never makes the
   total 0.  Consequently the first time the total becomes divisible by M is when
   Quentin himself plays a card satisfying Step Q1.

   If, improbably, no such card ever occurs earlier, the game eventually reaches
   the very last card.  Since Peggy moved first, Quentin moves last, and the sum
   of **all** 6k cards equals
         1 + 2 + \ldots  + 6k = 3k(6k + 1) = 3k\cdot M \equiv  0 (mod M),
   Quentin's final card necessarily makes the total 0 and he still wins.

6.  Conclusion
   Quentin (the second player) has a strategy that guarantees victory for every
   possible initial deal.  Therefore, under optimal play,

        Probability(Peggy wins) = 0.

core_steps: Card-counting: after every move by the first player, the second player still holds one extra card.
Pigeonhole: any card just played rules out at most one of the second player’s cards, so a ‘safe’ discard always exists for the second player.
Induction/iteration: the second player can therefore survive every round and is certain to make the final discard.
Arithmetic fact: the sum of all 2n card numbers is n(2n+1), which is 0 mod (2n+1).
Consequently the last (second) player wins with certainty ⇒ probability first player wins is 0.

slot1.description: size parameter for the deck (half the number of cards each player initially holds)

slot2.description: total number of cards in the deck, must be an even multiple of slot1

slot3.description: modulus that defines the winning divisibility condition; chosen so that the full-deck sum is 0 mod this number

slot4.description: labels/order of the two players (first vs. second); swapping them leaves the reasoning intact with roles reversed
